questions.question: Derive the Catalan number recurrence relation Cₙ = Σ(i=0 to n-1) CᵢCₙ₋₁₋ᵢ from the binary tree counting perspective.
keyPoints: Consider binary tree with root and left subtree of size i, right subtree of size n-1-i
For each i from 0 to n-1, count pairs of left and right subtrees
Total trees T(n) = Σ(i=0 to n-1) T(i) × T(n-1-i)
This is the convolution that produces Catalan numbers
Base case: C₀ = 1 (empty tree or tree with single node in shifted indexing)

questions.question: Prove that the number of full binary trees with n internal nodes is given by the nth Catalan number.
keyPoints: In a full binary tree, every internal node has exactly 2 children
If there are n internal nodes, there are n+1 leaves (L = I + 1)
Total nodes = 2n + 1
Consider root with left subtree having i internal nodes and right having n-1-i
Count: F(n) = Σ(i=0 to n-1) F(i)F(n-1-i), same as Catalan recurrence
Using combinatorial argument or generating function, F(n) = Cₙ

questions.question: Explain the relationship between binary tree counting and the average-case time complexity of BST operations.
keyPoints: BST search, insert, delete all take O(h) time where h is height
Random insertions give expected height O(log n)
Number of distinct BSTs with n keys = Cₙ (Catalan)
Average case analysis uses expected height over all Cₙ possible BST structures
This connects counting to probabilistic analysis of algorithms
Expected comparisons in random BST search ≈ 2 ln n ≈ 1.386 log₂ n

questions.question: Using the generating function method, derive the closed-form formula for Catalan numbers.
keyPoints: Define generating function C(x) = Σ(n≥0) Cₙxⁿ
From recurrence: C(x) = 1 + xC(x)² (constant term C₀=1)
Solve quadratic: C(x) = (1 - √(1-4x))/(2x)
Use binomial expansion: √(1-4x) = Σ(k≥0) (-1)ᵏ (4ˣ) (2k choose k) xᵏ / (2k choose k)
Coefficient extraction gives Cₙ = (1/(n+1)) (2n choose n) = (2n)!/(n!(n+1)!)

questions.question: Draw all possible binary tree structures with 4 nodes and verify the count equals C₄ = 14.
keyPoints: For n=4 nodes: Trees with 0,1,2,3 nodes in left subtree (and remaining in right)
Left size 0: Right has 3 nodes → C₀ × C₃ = 1×5 = 5 trees
Left size 1: Right has 2 nodes → C₁ × C₂ = 1×2 = 2 trees
Left size 2: Right has 1 node → C₂ × C₂ = 2×2 = 4 trees
Left size 3: Right has 0 nodes → C₃ × C₁ = 5×1 = 5 trees
Total = 5+2+4+5 = 14 = C₄. Draw each category to verify.

questions.question: Define and distinguish between: (i) Full binary tree (ii) Complete binary tree (iii) Perfect binary tree.
keyPoints: Full/Proper: Every node has 0 or 2 children; no node has exactly 1 child
Complete: All levels filled except possibly last; nodes are as left as possible
Perfect: Both full and complete; all leaf nodes at same level; has 2ᵏ⁻¹ - 1 nodes for height k-1
Perfect ⇒ Full ⇒ Complete (implications go one way)
Perfect: L = I + 1 and all levels full. Complete: may have incomplete last level but filled left-to-right.

questions.question: Show that the number of ways to parenthesize (n+1) factors using binary operators equals the nth Catalan number.
keyPoints: With n+1 factors and n binary operators, we need n binary operations
Each parenthesization corresponds to a binary expression tree with n internal nodes
The leaves are the n+1 factors, internal nodes are the operators
Number of distinct binary trees with n internal nodes = Cₙ
Thus number of parenthesizations = Cₙ = (1/(n+1))(2n choose n)
Example: (a+b)×(c+d) and ((a+b)×c)+d are two of C₂ = 2 parenthesizations for 3 factors

questions.question: Provide a comprehensive analysis of the combinatorial proof of the Catalan number formula using the Ballot Problem approach.
keyPoints: Ballot Problem: In an election with p votes for A and q votes for p>q, probability A is always ahead = (p-q)/(p+q)
Set p = n+1, q = n, then probability = 1/(2n+1)
Total ways to arrange 2n+1 votes (n+1 for A, n for B) = (2n+1)!/(n+1)!n!
Favorable sequences where A is always ahead = (1/(2n+1)) × (2n+1)!/(n+1)!n! = (2n)!/(n!(n+1)!) = Cₙ
This equals number of Dyck paths of semilength n (never go below axis)
Each Dyck path corresponds to a binary tree via up-step=left child, down-step=return
Thus binary tree count = Dyck path count = Cₙ = (2n)!/(n!(n+1)!)

questions.question: Develop an algorithm to generate all distinct binary tree structures with n nodes and analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Use recurrence: For each i from 0 to n-1, generate left subtree of size i and right of size n-1-i
Recursive approach: generateTree(n) calls generateTree(i) and generateTree(n-1-i) for all i
Base case: n=0 or n=1 returns single node
Algorithm enumerates all Cₙ trees through depth-first construction
Time complexity: O(Cₙ × n) to generate all trees (proportional to output size)
Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack, O(n) for storing each tree
Can be optimized using memoization or dynamic programming to compute counts in O(n²) time
The algorithm is inherently exponential in output size since Cₙ grows as ~4ⁿ/n^(3/2)
